request.question: Which entertainment company is the creator of "The X Factor" the chief executive officer?

title: Bobby Mehta
Jonathan G. Ornstein
Fredric Tomczyk
Live Nation Entertainment
X Factor (Belgian TV series)
Glen Post
Simon Cowell
Charles Bunch
Mike Lunsford
B. Wayne Hughes
sentences: Siddharth N. "Bobby" Mehta was former CEO and vice chairman of HSBC North America.
Mehta served as an Advisor of TransUnion since December 31, 2012.
Mehta serves as consultant of TransUnion.
He served the chief executive officer and president of TransUnion from August 2007 to December 31, 2012, and Transunion Financing Corp. until December 31, 2012.
From May 2007 to July 2007, he served as a consultant to the board of directors at TransUnion.
He served as the chief executive officer and president of TransUnion until December 31, 2012.
He served as the chief executive officer of TransUnion LLC.
He served as chairman of the board and chief executive officer of HSBC Finance Corporation from April 2005 to February 2007.
He served as chief executive officer and president of TransUnion LLC from 2007 to 2012.
From 1998 to 2007, he held a variety of positions with HSBC Finance Corporation and HSBC North America Holdings, Inc.
Mehta served as chief executive officer of HSBC North America until February 2007.
Mehta served as consultant of TransUnion since May 2007 until July 2007.
Mehta served as group managing director of HSBC Holdings PLC of HSBC Finance Corp. since April 30, 2005, and its unit chief executive officer since March 2005.
He served as the chief executive of HS BC North America Holdings Inc., of HSBC Finance Corp., from March 2005 to February 15, 2007.
He served as an executive chairman of HSBC Financial Corporation Limited since April 2005 and served as its chief executive officer from April 2005 to February 15, 2007.
He served as the chief executive officer of HSBC Bank USA, N.A. until February 2007.
He served as the chief executive officer of HSBC North America Holdings Inc. since March 2005.
He served as chairman and chief executive officer of HSBC Financial Corp., Ltd.
He oversaw HSBC's global credit card services, its North American consumer lending and mortgage services businesses and its first mortgage operation.

He was also responsible for corporate marketing, strategic planning and corporate development for HSBC North America Holdings Inc. and had responsibility for the strategic management of credit cards throughout the HSBC Group.
Mehta served as group executive of Credit Card Services, Auto Finance and Canada of Household International Inc., since July 2002.
He worked at MasterCard’s U.S. region board since March 2000.
Mehta joined Household International Inc., in 1998.
He served as senior vice president of The Boston Consulting Group in Los Angeles and co-leader of Boston Consulting Group Financial Services Practice in the United States.
Mehta served as a director of Global Board of MasterCard Incorporated since March 17, 2005.
He served as unit chairman of HSBC Holdings PLC and served as its board member since March 2005.
He served as vice chairman and director of HSBC Financial Corporation Limited., (Formerly Household International Inc.).
He has been a director of Avant Credit Corporation since December 18, 2014.
He has been an independent director of The Allstate Corporation since February 19, 2014.
He serves as a member of the advisory board at Core2 Group, Inc.
He has been non-executive independent director at Piramal Enterprises Ltd since April 1, 2013.
He serves on the boards of Datacard, Chicago Public Education Fund, University of Chicago Laboratory Schools, The Economic Club of Chicago, The Field Museum and Myelin Repair Foundation.
He serves as a director of TransUnion Corp. and TransUnion LLC.
He served as a director of MasterCard International Inc. (also known as MasterCard Worldwide) (formerly, MasterCard Inc.), since March 17, 2005.
He served as a director of HSBC Financial Corp.
Ltd.
He has been a director of TransUnion since April 2012.
Mehta serves on the board of international advisors for the Monterey, California, Institute of International Studies and is a member of the Financial Services Roundtable.
He also serves on the board of advisors for the Myelin Repair Foundation.
Mehta holds a Bachelor of Arts in economics from the London School of Economics and Masters of Business Administration from the University of Chicago.
He stepped down as head of the North American unit after the lender raised its forecast for bad loans in the U.S.
He is of Indian descent.
Jonathan Ornstein is Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Mesa Air Group, Inc., and was appointed effective May 1, 1998.
From April 1996 to his joining the company as Chief Executive Officer, Ornstein served as President and Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of Virgin Express, a European airline.
From 1995 to April 1996, Ornstein served as Chief Executive Officer of Virgin Express Holdings, Inc.
Ornstein joined Continental Express as President and Chief Executive Officer in July 1994 and, in November 1994, was named Senior Vice President, Airport Services at Continental Airlines.
Ornstein was previously employed by the company from 1988 to 1994, as Executive Vice President and as President of the company’s WestAir Holding, Inc., subsidiary.
Fredric Tomczyk was the president and chief executive officer of TD Ameritrade from 2008 to 2016.
He served on the company’s Board of Directors from January 2006 until June 2007.

Before that he served as vice chair of corporate operations for TD Bank Group, executive vice president of retail distribution for TD Canada Trust, and president and chief executive officer of wealth management for TD Bank.
Before joining TD Bank, he was president and chief executive officer of London Life Insurance Company.
Live Nation Entertainment is an American global entertainment company, formed from the merger of Live Nation and Ticketmaster.
It owns, leases, operates, has booking rights for and/or equity interests in a large number of U.S. entertainment venues.

The leadership consists of Greg Maffei (chief executive officer of Liberty Media) as chairman and Canadian Michael Rapino (previously chief executive officer of Live Nation) as president and CEO of the company.
X Factor is the Flemish version of the British show "The X Factor" that was created by Simon Cowell.
The new "X Factor" series was a postcursor to "Idool 2003" / "2004" which features contestants & jury members from both seasons.
Glen F. Post III (born October 4, 1952) is the chief executive officer and president of CenturyLink, an S&P 500 integrated communications service provider based out of Monroe, Louisiana.
He earned a bachelor's degree in accounting in 1974 at Louisiana Tech University and an MBA in 1976 at Louisiana Tech.
Post joined CenturyTel in 1976.
He was named vice president in 1982 and was promoted to senior vice president and treasurer in 1984.
He was appointed to the CenturyTel board of directors in 1985, and the following year he was promoted to senior vice president and chief financial officer.
In 1988 Post was named executive vice president and chief operating officer.
He became the president and chief operating officer of CenturyTel in 1990.
In 1992 Post was named vice chairman of the board, president, and chief executive officer.
In 2002 he was appointed chairman of the board and chief executive officer.
Since 2009 Post has served as chief executive officer and president of CenturyLink.

His honors include: Louisiana Tech College of Administration and Business Distinguished Alumni in 1991, Louisiana Tech University Tower Medallion Award in 1997 and DeGree Enterprises Lifetime Achievement Award in Business 2003.
Simon Phillip Cowell ( ) (born 7 October 1959) is an English reality television judge and producer, entrepreneur, and philanthropist.

He is most recognised as a judge on the British TV talent competition series "Pop Idol", "The X Factor", and "Britain's Got Talent", and the American TV talent competition shows "American Idol", "The X Factor," and "America's Got Talent".
Cowell is the principal founder and chief executive of the British entertainment company Syco.
Charles E. Bunch (born c. 1950) is an American businessman who served as the chairman and chief executive officer of PPG Industries, Inc., until succeeded by Michael H. McGarry on September 1, 2015.

Since 2002, he had been a director and prior to becoming president and chief executive officer in March 2005 and chairman and chief executive officer in July 2005, Bunch was president and chief operating officer from July 2002.
He was the executive vice president from 2000 to 2002 and senior vice president of Strategic Planning and Corporate Services from 1997 to 2000.
Bunch is also a director of the H. J. Heinz Company and The PNC Financial Services Group, Inc.
He attended Georgetown University as an undergraduate and received his MBA from Harvard University in 1979.
Mike Lunsford is the chief executive officer of SK Planet, Inc., the U.S. arm of SK Planet, Ltd., a Korean-based company.

He is the former executive vice president and interim chief executive officer of RealNetworks, the former chief executive officer of Rhapsody, a joint venture between RealNetworks and Viacom, and the former president and interim chief executive officer of Earthlink.
Before joining EarthLink, Lunsford worked as a consultant at Andersen Consulting (now Accenture) in Chicago and Scott, Madden & Associates, a management consulting firm in Raleigh, North Carolina.
He received an undergraduate degree and a Masters of Business Administration (MBA) from the University of North Carolina.
Bradley Wayne Hughes (born September 28, 1933) is the founder and chairman of Public Storage, the largest self-storage company in America doing business as a REIT or real estate investment trust.
As of 2014, Hughes is worth $2.2 billion.

Known all his life by his middle name, B. Wayne Hughes was the company's President and Co-Chief Executive Officer from 1980 until November 1991 when he became Chairman of the Board and sole Chief Executive Officer.
He retired as Chief Executive Officer in November 2002 and remains Chairman of the Board.
He was Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer from 1990 until March 1998 of Public Storage Properties XI, Inc., which was renamed PS Business Parks, Inc. ("PSB"), an affiliated REIT.

From 1989-90 until the respective dates of merger, he was Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer of 18 affiliated REITs that were merged into the Company between September 1994 and May 1998 (collectively, the "Merged Public Storage REITs").
has been active in the real estate investment field for over 30 years.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically COMPLETE given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

   - Narrowed trigger for absent question-named candidates: mark a named candidate as "absent and needing a page/bridge" only when (i) that question-named candidate is a semantically required argument to resolve the relation (the relation cannot be resolved without confirming a fact about that candidate), AND (ii) the supplied passages do not already contain any unambiguous short factual token (for example, an explicit date, direct property token, or relation-token) that would fully answer this hop. If the passages already include such an explicit token that resolves the relation, do NOT mark the hop INCOMPLETE merely because a candidate page is missing.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Apply this dynamic prioritization policy:
      a) If the missing fact is biographical or identity information about a semantically required question-named candidate (examples: birth date, occupation, nationality, canonical identity that the relation depends on), prioritize retrieving the candidate's canonical biography or main entity page first (exact title when known).
      b) If the missing fact is a property of another entity or a document type that typically holds the property (examples: game developer, layout engine used by a site, race event date, event report), prioritize a concise property-focused query or the canonical page that directly carries that property (examples: "[Game] developer", "[Browser] engine", "2009 Belgian Grand Prix").
      c) If name-disambiguation is suspected (same or similar names present on-page or multiple candidate pages could match), explicitly note the ambiguity and prioritize an exact candidate-title retrieval and a short disambiguation query (e.g., "<Name> (occupation)" or "<Name> disambiguation").
      d) Never prefer a biography-first retrieval when the missing item is better recovered by a targeted property query or when passages already include an explicit relation token that completes the hop.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding. Grounding snippets must be ≤12 words.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Construct the query as a single noun-phrase: a canonical anchor OR an anchor paired with a minimal disambiguator (anchor + short parenthetical/occupation/identifier) optionally followed by a single concise relation-noun when the missing bridge is a specific temporal or numeric attribute.
  - Only add a minimal disambiguator when the visible anchor is ambiguous and the disambiguator itself is explicitly present or strongly implied in `question` or `summary_1`. Do not add disambiguators when the anchor is already unambiguous or the bridge is already confirmed.
  - Relation additions are allowed only as a single neutral relation-noun that names the target attribute (examples: "birth year", "publication date", "species count"). Do NOT use verb-like or assertive relation phrasing (forbid words/phrases such as "born", "birth name", "aka", "founder", "founded", "is the").
  - The final query should look like: "Anchor" OR "Anchor Disambiguator" OR "Anchor Disambiguator relation-noun" (all as a short noun-phrase).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- Primary output: The summary's primary output should be the concise canonical label or token drawn exactly from `context` (do not change which label/name/token was selected by earlier answer-selection logic).

- When to append a supporting sentence: Append a single short, passage-grounded supporting sentence only when all of these three conditions hold:
  1) The `question`'s semantic intent genuinely requires a relation- or attribute-fact to justify or disambiguate the canonical label from `context` (not when the question is a pure identification/token-extraction or simple binary label with no disambiguating need).
  2) At least one retrieved `passage` contains an explicit, unambiguous predication that links the exact canonical label (or a clearly corroborated alias) to the required relation or attribute. The passage must state the link directly (examples of acceptable forms: 'X is a member of Y', 'X formed in YEAR', 'X was born DATE', 'X with Y', 'X is the Y of Z'). Mere co-occurrence, implication, or mention of the attribute elsewhere on the page is insufficient.
  3) Identity alignment is verified: confirm that the passage's assertion refers to the same entity token in `context` by matching the exact name or an alias that is explicitly corroborated in the passages (via roles, dates, titles, or other unambiguous attributes). If identity is ambiguous or the passage links the attribute to a different named entity, do not append.

- Content and form of the appended sentence: If appending, produce one short sentence that (a) explicitly names the canonical label or the corroborated alias and (b) states the direct relation/attribute exactly as asserted by the supporting passage (paraphrase briefly; do not invent or speculate). Keep the added sentence tightly focused and grounded in the passage wording; avoid long quotations. The entire `summary` (primary label plus appended sentence) must be concise and limited to 1–3 sentences total.

- When not to append: Do not append a supporting sentence and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply:
  - The question expects a normalized single-token identifier or binary/multiple-choice label and no passage-stated relation is required to disambiguate it.
  - The `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer for that question.
  - The supporting passage does not explicitly predicate the required relation/attribute of the same named entity/clearly corroborated alias in `context` (including cases of mere co-occurrence or indirect/mediated implication).
  - Identity alignment is ambiguous or ties the attribute to another entity.

- Transitive/alias caution: Do not collapse mediated or transitive links into a direct equivalence unless each necessary link is explicitly and unambiguously asserted in the provided passages and clearly refers to the same entity. If the relation would require chaining two separate assertions, both must be present and identity-aligned; otherwise refrain from asserting the transitive equivalence.

- Insufficient information: If `context` and `passages` together do not supply enough explicit, identity-aligned information to resolve the required relation/attribute, summarize the remaining uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

- Style and constraints: Keep output concise. Do not invent facts or add unsupported inferences. Avoid long verbatim quotations. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic. Produce only the `summary` field (a single string) as the integrated output.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

When summary_2 lists multiple co-referent lexical variants for the same entity, prefer the shortest explicit canonical variant only if all of the following hold: (1) that shorter variant appears verbatim in summary_2; (2) removing the longer form's additional qualifiers (parentheticals, locative suffixes, administrative/categorical words, punctuation/abbreviation markers) would not delete tokens the question explicitly requires or would not create ambiguity among entities visible in the summaries; and (3) the shorter variant still uniquely identifies the intended referent within the visible summaries. If any of these conditions fails, emit the form from summary_2 exactly, preserving parentheticals, punctuation, administrative/categorical tokens, multi-component place formatting, and parenthetical acronyms. Base these choices on semantic content and uniqueness in the visible evidence; do not apply surface heuristics that blindly strip parentheses, punctuation, locative suffixes, legal or category words, or other disambiguating tokens.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

Cowell is the principal founder and chief executive of the British entertainment company Syco.
Charles E. Bunch (born c. 1950) is an American businessman who served as the chairman and chief executive officer of PPG Industries, Inc., until succeeded by Michael H. McGarry on September 1, 2015.

Since 2002, he had been a director and prior to becoming president and chief executive officer in March 2005 and chairman and chief executive officer in July 2005, Bunch was president and chief operating officer from July 2002.
He was the executive vice president from 2000 to 2002 and senior vice president of Strategic Planning and Corporate Services from 1997 to 2000.
Bunch is also a director of the H. J. Heinz Company and The PNC Financial Services Group, Inc.
He attended Georgetown University as an undergraduate and received his MBA from Harvard University in 1979.
Mike Lunsford is the chief executive officer of SK Planet, Inc., the U.S. arm of SK Planet, Ltd., a Korean-based company.

He is the former executive vice president and interim chief executive officer of RealNetworks, the former chief executive officer of Rhapsody, a joint venture between RealNetworks and Viacom, and the former president and interim chief executive officer of Earthlink.
Before joining EarthLink, Lunsford worked as a consultant at Andersen Consulting (now Accenture) in Chicago and Scott, Madden & Associates, a management consulting firm in Raleigh, North Carolina.
He received an undergraduate degree and a Masters of Business Administration (MBA) from the University of North Carolina.
Bradley Wayne Hughes (born September 28, 1933) is the founder and chairman of Public Storage, the largest self-storage company in America doing business as a REIT or real estate investment trust.
As of 2014, Hughes is worth $2.2 billion.

Known all his life by his middle name, B. Wayne Hughes was the company's President and Co-Chief Executive Officer from 1980 until November 1991 when he became Chairman of the Board and sole Chief Executive Officer.
He retired as Chief Executive Officer in November 2002 and remains Chairman of the Board.
He was Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer from 1990 until March 1998 of Public Storage Properties XI, Inc., which was renamed PS Business Parks, Inc. ("PSB"), an affiliated REIT.

From 1989-90 until the respective dates of merger, he was Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer of 18 affiliated REITs that were merged into the Company between September 1994 and May 1998 (collectively, the "Merged Public Storage REITs").
has been active in the real estate investment field for over 30 years.

response.hop1_query: Which entertainment company is the creator of "The X Factor" the chief executive officer?
hop1_docs: Live Nation Entertainment | Live Nation Entertainment is an American global entertainment company, formed from the merger of Live Nation and Ticketmaster.  It owns, leases, operates, has booking rights for and/or equity interests in a large number of U.S. entertainment venues.  The leadership consists of Greg Maffei (chief executive officer of Liberty Media) as chairman and Canadian Michael Rapino (previously chief executive officer of Live Nation) as president and CEO of the company.

Rod Roddenberry | Eugene Wesley "Rod" Roddenberry Jr. (born February 5, 1974) is an American television producer and the chief executive officer of Roddenberry Entertainment.  Eugene, the son of "Star Trek" creator Gene Roddenberry and Majel Barrett, is an executive producer on "", the first episode of which was released on September 24, 2017.

En Masse Entertainment | En Masse Entertainment, Inc. (formerly Bluehole Interactive, Inc.) is an American video game publisher and a subsidiary of South Korean video game developer Bluehole.  Founded in June 2008, the company is based in Seattle, Washington and is best-known for publishing massively multiplayer online role-playing game "TERA", which was developed by parent company Bluehole.  The original En Masse leadership team had extensive experience in the video game industry at leading companies, including Blizzard Entertainment, Microsoft, NCsoft, ArenaNet, and Electronic Arts.  In January 2013, Chris Lee and Soo Min Park became chief executive officer and chief operating officer, respectively.
Essgee Entertainment | Essgee Entertainment is a professional performing and publishing company formed in 1981 in Australia.  Its founder and chief executive officer is entertainer Simon Gallaher.

Hothead Games | Hothead Games Inc. is an independent Canadian video game developer based in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada.  The studio was founded in 2006 by Steve Bocska, Vlad Ceraldi and Joel DeYoung, all three of which were formerly employed by Radical Entertainment.  However, Bocska left the company again in 2007.  On 10 March 2009, Ian Wilkinson, who had been president and chief executive officer of Radical Entertainment since its foundation in 1991 until 2008, became president and chief executive officer of Hothead Games, replacing Ceraldi.  In turn, Ceraldi became the studio's director of game development, while DeYoung moved from his chief operating officer position to director of game technology.

Todd Miller (media executive) | Todd Miller is an American-born media executive and the Chief Executive Officer of Celestial Tiger Entertainment (CTE), a diversified media company headquartered in Hong Kong.  The company creates and distributes branded television services and is a venture between Saban Capital Group; Celestial Pictures, a company owned by Astro Overseas Limited; and Lionsgate, the world's largest independent filmed entertainment studio.

VOC Opperhoofden in Japan | "Opperhoofd" is a Dutch word (plural "opperhoofden") which literally means 'supreme head[man]'.  In its historical usage, the word is a gubernatorial title, comparable to the English chief factor, for the chief executive officer of a Dutch "factory" in the sense of trading post, as led by a factor, i.e. agent.  The Japanese called the Dutch chief factors "kapitan" (from Portuguese "capitão").

hop2_docs: Bart Thompson | Bart Thompson (born September 20 in Los Angeles), is the creator of the comic book company Approbation Comics, and is the creator, writer, and artist of titles such as "Vampires Unlimited", the "Metamutoids", "ChiSai", "The Lazarus Factor", "Chaos Campus", and "Weapons of Mass Destruction".  Thompson is the scripter for the first volume of the comic book Lethal Instinct for Alias Enterprises and the writer for the remainder of the series.  Thompson also writes and edits the series "Blood, Shells, & Roses" for Arcana Studios.

Cheetos | Cheetos (formerly styled as Chee-tos until 1998) is a brand of cheese-flavored, puffed cornmeal snacks made by Frito-Lay, a subsidiary of PepsiCo.  Fritos creator Charles Elmer Doolin invented Cheetos in 1948, and began national distribution in the U.S.  The initial success of Cheetos was a contributing factor to the merger between The Frito Company and H.W. Lay & Company in 1961 to form Frito-Lay.  In 1965 Frito-Lay became a subsidiary of The Pepsi-Cola Company, forming PepsiCo, the current owner of the Cheetos brand.

Goldman Sachs asset management factor model | Goldman Sachs asset management (GSAM) factor model is one of the quantitative/ factor models used by financial analysts to assess the performance and financial condition of a company.  Typically quantitative models are based on inputs obtained from financial statements(FS).  There are various types of factor models – statistical models, macroeconomic models and fundamental models.  A fundamental factor model uses company and industry attributes and market data known as "factors" to explain a company's historical returns.  Since the input factors from FS may be questionable or the data may not be comparable over time this model includes a factor that is based on an assessment by equity analysts performing traditional equity analysis.

Max Factor | Max Factor is a popular line of cosmetics company from Coty, Inc..  It was founded in 1909 as Max Factor & Company by Maksymilian Faktorowicz, a beautician from Poland.  In its early days, it specialized in movie make-up.  Until its 1973 sale for US$2.7 billion, Max Factor & Company was owned by several generations of the family, becoming an international company during that time.  Procter & Gamble purchased it in 1991, and it remains a popular brand.

Joe Quesada | Joseph "Joe" Quesada ( ; born January 12, 1962) is an American comic book editor, writer and artist.  He became known in the 1990s for his work on various Valiant Comics books, such as "Ninjak" and "Solar, Man of the Atom".  He later worked on numerous books for DC Comics and Marvel Comics, such as "" and "X-Factor", before forming his own company, Event Comics, where he published his creator-owned character, Ash.

Larry Stroman | Larry Stroman is an American comic book artist and writer.  After first gaining attention illustrating the Epic Comics series "Alien Legion" (vol.  2), he went on to illustrate various Marvel Comics books such as "The Uncanny X-Men", "X-Factor", "Ghost Rider" (vol.  2) and "Punisher", as well as work for other companies, such as DC Comics' "Darkstars" and Dark Horse Comics' "The Mark".  He briefly published a creator-owned series called "The Tribe" with Image Comics in the early 1990s.

The Game Creators | The Game Creators, Ltd. (often stylised "tgc") is a British game development software company formed through a partnership between programmer Lee Bamber and Rick Vanner in 1999, located in Macclesfield, Cheshire, United Kingdom.  The company was formerly known as "Dark Basic Software Limited".  The company primarily develops and markets game development tools for Windows.  Among others, it has developed DarkBASIC Professional, its predecessor DarkBASIC, App Game Kit, FPS Creator (and its successors FPS Creator X10 and GameGuru) and The 3D Gamemaker.
